questions.ncert_line: The cohesion-tension theory (Dixon and Joly) explains ascent of sap. Water molecules are held together by cohesion forces and to cell walls by adhesion. Transpiration from leaves creates tension (negative pressure) that pulls water up through xylem.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.2: Ascent of Sap
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.2: Ascent of Sap

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question:
Assertion (A): Transport in phloem is bidirectional (can move up or down).
Reason (R): Phloem transports sugars from source (leaf) to sink (roots/fruits) according to the pressure flow hypothesis.

options.A: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true but R is false
options.D: A is false but R is true

questions.ncert_line: The pressure flow hypothesis (Ernst Münch) explains phloem transport. High sugar concentration at source (leaf) creates high osmotic pressure; water moves in → high turgor pressure → pushes sap toward sink (root/fruit). The flow is bidirectional in phloem.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.3: Phloem Transport
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.4.3: Phloem Transport

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Short day plants flower when the duration of darkness exceeds a critical period. The photoreceptor involved in this response is:
questions.ncert_line: Photoperiodism is the response of plants to the relative duration of light and darkness. Short day plants (SDP) flower when the night period exceeds a critical length. Long day plants (LDP) flower when the night period is below the critical length. The photoreceptor is phytochrome.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.5: Photoperiodism
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.5: Photoperiodism

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Vernalisation refers to the promotion of flowering by:
questions.ncert_line: Vernalisation is the acceleration of flowering by a period of low temperature. It is the cold treatment requirement for flowering in some plants (e.g., winter wheat, biennial plants like sugar beet). It prevents precocious reproductive development.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.6: Vernalisation
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 15 — Plant Growth and Development, Section 15.6: Vernalisation

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The net yield of ATP per glucose molecule in glycolysis is:
questions.ncert_line: Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm and produces 2 pyruvate, 2 ATP (net), and 2 NADH from one glucose molecule. Under aerobic conditions, pyruvate enters the mitochondria. The Krebs cycle produces 3 NADH, 1 FADH₂, 1 GTP per acetyl-CoA turn.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.2–14.4
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.2–14.4

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The respiratory quotient (RQ) for the complete oxidation of glucose (carbohydrate) is:
questions.ncert_line: Respiratory quotient (RQ) = CO₂ released / O₂ consumed. For carbohydrates, RQ = 1. For fats, RQ < 1 (about 0.7) as fats are more reduced and need more O₂. For organic acids, RQ > 1. RQ for succulent plants undergoing CAM is ≈ 0 at night.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.6: Respiratory Quotient
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.6: Respiratory Quotient

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following does NOT cause stomatal closure?
questions.ncert_line: Stomata open when guard cells become turgid and close when they become flaccid. Stomatal opening is triggered by: light, low CO₂ concentration, and K⁺ accumulation in guard cells. ABA (abscisic acid) promotes stomatal closure.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.3.3: Stomatal Transpiration
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 11 — Transport in Plants, Section 11.3.3: Stomatal Transpiration

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which enzyme converts starch to maltose in the mouth?
questions.ncert_line: Salivary amylase (ptyalin) digests starch to maltose. Pepsin in stomach digests proteins. Trypsin (from pancreas) digests proteins. Lipase (from pancreas) digests fats. Lactase digests lactose. The intestinal juice contains maltase, sucrase, lactase and peptidases.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.2–16.3
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.2–16.3

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: After absorption in intestinal cells, fats are transported via chylomicrons into the:
questions.ncert_line: Glucose and amino acids are absorbed by active transport. Fatty acids and glycerol are absorbed by diffusion and re-synthesised into lipoproteins (chylomicrons) in intestinal cells. Fats enter the lymph (lacteals) via chylomicrons, NOT directly into the blood.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.4: Absorption of Digested Products
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.4: Absorption of Digested Products

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The normal RBC count in human blood is approximately:
questions.ncert_line: Blood is composed of plasma (55%) and formed elements (45%). Plasma contains water (90-92%), proteins (fibrinogen, globulins, albumins). RBCs (erythrocytes) contain haemoglobin. Normal RBC count: 5 million/mm³. Platelets (thrombocytes) help in clotting.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.1: Blood
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.1: Blood

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The natural pacemaker of the human heart is the:
questions.ncert_line: The human heart has four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle. The right side pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs (pulmonary circuit). The left side pumps oxygenated blood to the body (systemic circuit). SA node is the pacemaker.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.3: Heart
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.3: Heart

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following distinguishes lymph from blood plasma?

options.C: Lymph has fewer proteins and no RBCs

questions.ncert_line: Lymph is a colourless fluid similar to plasma but with less proteins and no RBCs. The lymph nodes are present along lymphatic vessels. Lymph returns proteins and fluids to the blood. It carries chylomicrons (fat) absorbed from the intestine into the blood.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.6: Lymphatic System
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.6: Lymphatic System

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: What percentage of the 180 litres of glomerular filtrate formed daily is reabsorbed back into the blood?
questions.ncert_line: Urine formation involves three processes: ultrafiltration (at Bowman's capsule), selective reabsorption (along tubules), and tubular secretion. About 180 litres of filtrate forms per day, but only 1-2 litres of urine is excreted (99% reabsorption). ADH promotes water reabsorption.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.2: Mechanism of Urine Formation
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.2: Mechanism of Urine Formation

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) secretes renin in response to:
questions.ncert_line: The juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA) is a specialised structure formed by the macula densa (modified cells of DCT) and juxtaglomerular cells (modified smooth muscle cells of afferent arteriole). JGA secretes renin when blood pressure falls, activating the renin-angiotensin system.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.4: Regulation of Kidney Function
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 19 — Excretory Products, Section 19.4: Regulation of Kidney Function

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: According to the sliding filament theory, muscle contraction is initiated when calcium ions bind to:
questions.ncert_line: According to the sliding filament theory, muscle contraction occurs when actin filaments slide over myosin filaments. Cross bridges form between myosin heads and actin. ATP hydrolysis provides energy. Calcium ions (released from SR) initiate contraction by binding to troponin.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.2.2: Mechanism of Muscle Contraction
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.2.2: Mechanism of Muscle Contraction

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The total number of bones in the adult human skeleton is:
questions.ncert_line: The human skeleton has 206 bones. The axial skeleton (80 bones) includes skull, vertebral column, sternum and ribs. The appendicular skeleton (126 bones) includes limbs and girdles. Joints can be fibrous (immovable), cartilaginous (slightly movable) or synovial (freely movable).
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.3: Skeletal System
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 20 — Locomotion and Movement, Section 20.3: Skeletal System

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which division of the autonomic nervous system is responsible for the 'fight or flight' response?
questions.ncert_line: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the brain and spinal cord. The peripheral nervous system (PNS) consists of cranial and spinal nerves. The autonomic nervous system (ANS) has sympathetic (fight or flight) and parasympathetic (rest and digest) divisions.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.4: Central Neural System
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.4: Central Neural System

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The semicircular canals in the inner ear are primarily responsible for:

options.D: Converting sound waves to nerve impulses

questions.ncert_line: The human ear has three parts: outer ear (pinna and auditory canal), middle ear (tympanic membrane, malleus, incus, stapes), and inner ear (cochlea and vestibular apparatus). The cochlea is for hearing; semicircular canals are for balance.
questions.ncert_section: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.3.2: Ear
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control, Section 21.3.2: Ear

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which microorganism is used in the commercial production of acetic acid (vinegar)?
questions.ncert_line: Lactobacillus and other lactic acid bacteria (LAB) help in curd formation — they convert lactose to lactic acid. Baker's yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) is used in bread making. Aspergillus niger produces citric acid. Acetobacter aceti produces acetic acid (vinegar).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.1: Microbes in Household Products
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.1: Microbes in Household Products

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming from the fungus:
questions.ncert_line: Penicillin was discovered by Alexander Fleming from Penicillium notatum (now P. chrysogenum). It was the first antibiotic. Streptomycin is produced by Streptomyces griseus. Chloramphenicol is produced by Streptomyces venezuelae.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.2: Microbes in Industrial Products
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.2: Microbes in Industrial Products

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) is used to measure:

options.A: Amount of dissolved oxygen in water
options.B: Degree of organic pollution in water

questions.ncert_line: Primary treatment of sewage removes floating and suspended solid matter by filtration and sedimentation. Secondary (biological) treatment uses microbes to break down organic matter — the BOD (biochemical oxygen demand) is reduced. Activated sludge contains aerobic microbes.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.5: Microbes in Sewage Treatment
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.5: Microbes in Sewage Treatment

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Baculoviruses are used as biocontrol agents because they are:

options.B: Species-specific pathogens that spare other organisms

questions.ncert_line: Baculoviruses (Nuclear Polyhedrosis Viruses — NPV) are species-specific pathogens used as biocontrol agents against insects and arthropods. Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is a biocontrol agent against butterfly caterpillars. Trichoderma is used to control plant fungal pathogens.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.3: Microbes in Biocontrol
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.3: Microbes in Biocontrol

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The main component of biogas produced in a biogas plant is:
questions.ncert_line: Biogas (mainly methane) is produced by anaerobic bacteria (methanogens) from biomass. Methanobacterium is a methanogenic archaebacterium found in rumen of cattle. Biogas plants decompose cattle dung (gobar gas) anaerobically to produce biogas.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.4: Microbes as Biofuel
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.4: Microbes as Biofuel

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is essential in a cloning vector for identifying successful recombinants?
questions.ncert_line: A cloning vector must have: an origin of replication (ori), a selectable marker (antibiotic resistance gene), and cloning sites (restriction enzyme recognition sites). Plasmids, bacteriophages (λ phage) and cosmids are commonly used vectors.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.2: Cloning Vectors
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.2: Cloning Vectors

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first recombinant human insulin (Humulin) was produced commercially in the bacterium:
questions.ncert_line: Recombinant human insulin (Humulin) was the first commercially produced recombinant protein. It was produced in E. coli. The insulin gene was produced from two separate chains (A and B) with C-peptide linking them. Eli Lilly produced it commercially in 1982.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.2: Genetically Engineered Insulin
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.2: Genetically Engineered Insulin

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: RNA interference (RNAi) involves gene silencing using:
questions.ncert_line: RNA interference (RNAi) is a mechanism of gene silencing using dsRNA (double-stranded RNA). Nematode-resistant tobacco was developed using RNAi. The dsRNA initiates RNAi in host cells, preventing translation of specific mRNA, providing protection against nematodes.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.3: RNA Interference
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and Its Applications, Section 12.2.3: RNA Interference

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Agrobacterium tumefaciens is used in plant genetic engineering because:

options.A: It can fix nitrogen in plant cells
options.B: Its Ti plasmid can transfer T-DNA into the plant genome
options.C: It produces antibiotics that select for transformed cells
options.D: It has restriction enzymes compatible with plant DNA

questions.ncert_line: Agrobacterium tumefaciens is a soil bacterium that causes crown gall disease in plants. Its Ti (tumour-inducing) plasmid can integrate a segment of its DNA (T-DNA) into the plant genome. It is used as a vector to introduce foreign genes into plant cells.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.5: Agrobacterium as Vector
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology: Principles and Processes, Section 11.1.5: Agrobacterium as Vector

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The final step in decomposition that releases inorganic nutrients from humus is called:
questions.ncert_line: Decomposition is carried out by bacteria and fungi (decomposers) on dead organic matter (detritus). The steps are: fragmentation (by detritivores), leaching, catabolism, humification (dark-coloured humus formation) and mineralisation.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.3: Decomposition
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.3: Decomposition

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The final stable community that develops at the end of ecological succession is called the:
questions.ncert_line: Primary succession begins in bare areas (bare rock, pond, sand dune). The pioneer community modifies the environment making it suitable for more complex communities. Each successive community is called a seral community. The final stable community is the climax community.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.6: Ecological Succession
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.6: Ecological Succession

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: India has three biodiversity hotspots. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
questions.ncert_line: Biodiversity hotspots are regions with high levels of endemic species and high levels of habitat loss. India has three hotspots: the Western Ghats and Sri Lanka, the Eastern Himalayas, and the Indo-Burma region.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4.2: Hotspots
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4.2: Hotspots

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is an ex situ conservation method?
questions.ncert_line: In situ conservation means conserving species in their natural habitats (national parks, wildlife sanctuaries, biosphere reserves). Ex situ conservation is off-site conservation in zoos, botanical gardens, seed banks and cryopreservation. Seed banks preserve seeds at −196°C (liquid nitrogen).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4: Conservation of Biodiversity
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.4: Conservation of Biodiversity

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In the logistic growth model, the population growth rate is maximum when the population size N equals:
questions.ncert_line: Population growth can be exponential (J-shaped, dN/dt = rN) or logistic (S-shaped, dN/dt = rN(K−N)/K). The logistic growth has a carrying capacity K. The inflection point of logistic growth is at N = K/2 where growth rate is maximum.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.2.1: Growth Models
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.2.1: Growth Models

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The international agreement to phase out CFC production to protect the ozone layer is called the:
questions.ncert_line: Ozone (O₃) in the stratosphere absorbs harmful UV-B radiation. CFCs (chlorofluorocarbons) released from refrigerants and aerosols destroy the ozone layer. The ozone hole is most prominent over Antarctica. The Montreal Protocol (1987) banned CFCs.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.5: Ozone Depletion
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.5: Ozone Depletion

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Eutrophication refers to:

options.A: Depletion of oxygen in water bodies
options.B: Excessive nutrient enrichment causing algal bloom in water bodies
options.C: Accumulation of heavy metals in water

questions.ncert_line: The full form of BOD is Biochemical Oxygen Demand — it is the amount of oxygen needed by bacteria to decompose organic matter in water. High BOD indicates high organic pollution. Eutrophication is excessive growth of phytoplankton due to enrichment of nutrients in water bodies.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.2: Water Pollution
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 16 — Environmental Issues, Section 16.2: Water Pollution

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which nutrient cycle does NOT have a significant atmospheric component?
questions.ncert_line: In the carbon cycle, carbon enters living systems through photosynthesis and returns to the atmosphere through respiration, decomposition and combustion. Phosphorus cycle has no atmospheric component — it cycles from soil to organisms and back. Nitrogen cycle involves fixation, nitrification, assimilation, ammonification and denitrification.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.5: Nutrient Cycling
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.5: Nutrient Cycling

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Apomixis refers to:

options.A: Fusion of two gametes without fertilisation

questions.ncert_line: Apomixis is the production of seeds without fertilisation. It occurs by: development from diploid egg cell (diplospory), development from nucellus/integument cells (apospory), or vegetative apomixis (adventive embryony). Apomixis is common in Asteraceae and grasses.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.7.3: Apomixis
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.7.3: Apomixis

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Syphilis is caused by:
questions.ncert_line: STIs (sexually transmitted infections) include bacterial infections (gonorrhoea — Neisseria gonorrhoeae; syphilis — Treponema pallidum) and viral infections (AIDS — HIV; genital herpes — HSV-2; hepatitis B — HBV; genital warts — HPV). Trichomoniasis is caused by protozoan Trichomonas vaginalis.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.4: Sexually Transmitted Infections
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.4: Sexually Transmitted Infections

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which ART procedure are the gametes (not the embryo) transferred into the fallopian tube?
questions.ncert_line: Assisted Reproductive Technologies include: IVF-ET (in vitro fertilisation and embryo transfer), GIFT (gamete intrafallopian transfer), ICSI (intracytoplasmic sperm injection), ZIFT (zygote intrafallopian transfer). The test tube baby procedure uses IVF and ET.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.5: Assisted Reproductive Technologies
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.5: Assisted Reproductive Technologies

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In which organism is sex determined by the ZW-ZZ system (females are ZW)?
questions.ncert_line: In humans, sex is determined by XX-XY system (male = XY). In grasshopper, male = XO (45 chromosomes). In birds, female = ZW and male = ZZ. In honey bees, males (drones) are haploid and females (workers/queen) are diploid — haplo-diploidy.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.7: Sex Determination
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.7: Sex Determination

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Genes that are close together on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together. This phenomenon is called:
questions.ncert_line: Linked genes tend to be inherited together because they are on the same chromosome. Morgan showed that genes close together on a chromosome show less crossing over (lower recombination frequency). The percentage of recombination ≈ map distance in centimorgans (cM).
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.8: Linkage and Crossing Over
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.8: Linkage and Crossing Over

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A mutation involving a change in a single base pair of DNA is called a:
questions.ncert_line: A point mutation involves a change in a single base pair. Frame-shift mutations (insertion/deletion) alter the reading frame from the point of mutation. Transition substitutes a purine for a purine (or pyrimidine for pyrimidine). Transversion substitutes a purine for a pyrimidine or vice versa.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.10: Mutation
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.10: Mutation

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In an autosomal recessive trait, two unaffected parents can produce an affected offspring. This means both parents are:
questions.ncert_line: Pedigree analysis is used to determine the pattern of inheritance of a trait. Autosomal dominant traits: appear in every generation, unaffected parents cannot have affected offspring. Autosomal recessive: can skip generations; can appear in offspring of two unaffected carriers.
questions.ncert_section: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.9: Pedigree Analysis
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance, Section 5.9: Pedigree Analysis

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a criterion used by Whittaker for five kingdom classification?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.1
questions.ncert_line: Whittaker used cell structure, mode of nutrition, body organisation, phylogenetic relationships and reproduction.
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Mode of reproduction is NOT one of Whittaker's five criteria.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Cyanobacteria are placed in Kingdom Monera because they are:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.1.1
questions.ncert_line: Monerans are characterised by prokaryotic organisation — no membrane-bound nucleus or organelles.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Cyanobacteria are prokaryotes — the defining feature of Kingdom Monera.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Mycoplasma can survive without oxygen and are found in soil and sewage. They are unique because:

options.A: They have a rigid cell wall made of chitin
options.B: They lack a cell wall entirely
options.C: They reproduce only by binary fission

questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 2 — Biological Classification, Section 2.1.1
questions.ncert_line: Mycoplasma are the smallest living cells and are unique in lacking a cell wall completely.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Mycoplasma lack a cell wall — making them resistant to penicillin and highly pleomorphic.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The reserve food material in Rhodophyceae (red algae) is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.1
questions.ncert_line: Rhodophyceae store food as floridean starch which is similar to amylopectin and glycogen.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Floridean starch is the stored food in red algae. Laminarin/mannitol = brown algae.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following statements about Bryophytes is INCORRECT?

options.A: They are called amphibians of the plant kingdom
options.D: The dominant phase is the gametophyte

questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 3 — Plant Kingdom, Section 3.2
questions.ncert_line: Bryophytes lack vascular tissue (xylem and phloem). They are non-vascular plants.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Bryophytes do NOT have vascular tissue — that is the key feature of pteridophytes onwards.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The water vascular system in Echinoderms is used for:

options.B: Locomotion, capture of food and respiration

questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.1
questions.ncert_line: The water vascular system of echinoderms is a unique feature used for locomotion, capture of food and respiration.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Water vascular system serves locomotion, food capture and respiration in echinoderms.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: A student observes an animal with a notochord only in the larval stage. The animal belongs to:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 4 — Animal Kingdom, Section 4.2
questions.ncert_line: In Urochordata (tunicates), the notochord is present only in the larval tail — hence the name.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Urochordata have notochord only in larval stage. Cephalochordata retain it throughout.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The fluid mosaic model of plasma membrane was proposed by:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.2
questions.ncert_line: The fluid mosaic model was proposed by S.J. Singer and G.L. Nicolson in 1972.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Singer and Nicolson (1972) proposed the fluid mosaic model of cell membrane.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following organelles is NOT bounded by a membrane?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 8 — Cell: The Unit of Life, Section 8.3
questions.ncert_line: Ribosomes are non-membranous organelles made of RNA and protein. They are 70S in prokaryotes and 80S in eukaryotes.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ribosomes have NO membrane. All others (mitochondria, nucleus, vacuole) are membrane-bound.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During which phase of mitosis do chromosomes appear to be shortest and thickest?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.2
questions.ncert_line: At metaphase, chromosomes are maximally condensed and shortest-thickest. The spindle is fully formed.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Metaphase — chromosomes are most condensed, best for karyotyping.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The stage of meiosis in which crossing over takes place is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 10 — Cell Cycle and Cell Division, Section 10.3
questions.ncert_line: Crossing over (exchange of segments between non-sister chromatids) occurs during pachytene of prophase I.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Pachytene — crossing over occurs. Zygotene = synapsis. Diplotene = chiasmata visible.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In C4 plants, the primary CO2 acceptor is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants, Section 13.4
questions.ncert_line: In C4 pathway, CO2 is fixed by PEP carboxylase in mesophyll cells. PEP is the primary acceptor.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. PEP (phosphoenolpyruvate) is the primary CO2 acceptor in C4 plants.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a product of the light reactions of photosynthesis?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 13 — Photosynthesis in Higher Plants, Section 13.2
questions.ncert_line: Light reactions produce ATP, NADPH and O2. Glucose is produced in the dark reactions (Calvin cycle).
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Glucose is NOT a product of light reactions — it is made in the Calvin cycle (dark reactions).

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The net gain of ATP molecules in glycolysis per glucose molecule is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.1
questions.ncert_line: Glycolysis produces 4 ATP but uses 2 ATP, giving a net gain of 2 ATP per glucose.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Net ATP from glycolysis = 4 produced − 2 used = 2 ATP.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: During anaerobic respiration in yeast, pyruvate is converted to:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 14 — Respiration in Plants, Section 14.2
questions.ncert_line: In yeast, pyruvate is converted to acetaldehyde (by pyruvate decarboxylase) then to ethanol (by alcohol dehydrogenase), releasing CO2.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Yeast fermentation: pyruvate → ethanol + CO2. Lactic acid is produced in animal muscle cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which enzyme converts milk protein casein into paracasein in the stomach?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 16 — Digestion and Absorption, Section 16.2
questions.ncert_line: Rennin (chymosin) is a proteolytic enzyme found in gastric juice of infants that acts on casein.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Rennin converts casein to paracasein. It is present in the stomach of infants.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The partial pressure of CO2 (pCO2) in alveolar air is approximately:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 17 — Breathing and Exchange of Gases, Section 17.3
questions.ncert_line: pCO2 in alveolar air = 40 mm Hg. In deoxygenated blood = 45 mm Hg. This gradient drives CO2 into alveoli.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. pCO2 in alveolar air = 40 mm Hg. pO2 in alveolar air = 104 mm Hg.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The P-wave in an ECG represents:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 18 — Body Fluids and Circulation, Section 18.3
questions.ncert_line: P-wave = atrial depolarisation (leads to atrial contraction). QRS = ventricular depolarisation. T-wave = ventricular repolarisation.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. P-wave = atrial depolarisation. QRS complex = ventricular depolarisation. T-wave = ventricular repolarisation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The structural and functional unit of the nervous system is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control and Coordination, Section 21.1
questions.ncert_line: The neuron (nerve cell) is the structural and functional unit of the nervous system.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Neuron is the structural and functional unit. Neuroglia are supporting cells.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the refractory period in a nerve fibre?

options.A: Period during which the nerve fires maximally
options.B: Period during which the nerve cannot generate another action potential

questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Biology, Chapter 21 — Neural Control and Coordination, Section 21.2
questions.ncert_line: The refractory period is the time after an action potential during which the nerve cannot generate another impulse.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Refractory period = nerve cannot fire again. Ensures one-way propagation of impulse.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: In a testcross, the test individual is crossed with:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 5 — Principles of Inheritance and Variation, Section 5.1
questions.ncert_line: A testcross involves crossing the individual of unknown genotype with a homozygous recessive parent.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Testcross = unknown genotype × homozygous recessive. Reveals whether unknown is AA or Aa.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The Okazaki fragments are joined together by which enzyme?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.3
questions.ncert_line: DNA ligase seals the nicks between Okazaki fragments on the lagging strand by forming phosphodiester bonds.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. DNA ligase joins Okazaki fragments. DNA polymerase I removes RNA primers first.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The codon AUG codes for:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 6 — Molecular Basis of Inheritance, Section 6.5
questions.ncert_line: AUG is the start codon that codes for methionine (Met). It initiates translation in all mRNAs.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. AUG = methionine = start codon. UAA, UAG, UGA are stop codons.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Stanley Miller's experiment demonstrated the formation of amino acids from:

options.A: CH4, NH3, H2O and H2 under electric discharge
options.B: CO2, N2, H2O under UV radiation
options.C: CH4, O2, H2O under high temperature
options.D: CO, NH3, H2S under volcanic conditions

questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 7 — Evolution, Section 7.2
questions.ncert_line: Miller used CH4, NH3, H2O and H2 (simulating early earth) with electric discharge to produce amino acids.
questions.explanation: Correct: A. Miller (1953) used CH4 + NH3 + H2O + H2 with electric discharge → amino acids.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Natural selection acts on:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 7 — Evolution, Section 7.4
questions.ncert_line: Natural selection acts on the phenotype (observable characteristics) not directly on the genotype.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Natural selection acts on phenotype. The genotype is exposed to selection only through phenotype.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of commensalism?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 13 — Organisms and Populations, Section 13.3
questions.ncert_line: Barnacles on whales is commensalism — barnacles benefit (food, transport) while whales are unaffected.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Barnacles on whales = commensalism (+/0). Mycorrhizae = mutualism. Plasmodium = parasitism.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The pyramid of energy is always upright because:

options.B: Energy is always lost at each trophic level

questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 14 — Ecosystem, Section 14.3
questions.ncert_line: Energy is lost as heat at every trophic level (10% rule). So energy always decreases up the food chain.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Pyramid of energy is ALWAYS upright — energy lost at each level ensures producers have most.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The term 'biodiversity' was popularised by:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.1
questions.ncert_line: The term 'biological diversity' was popularised by sociobiologist E.O. Wilson in 1992.
questions.explanation: Correct: A. E.O. Wilson popularised 'biodiversity'. Humboldt studied species-area relationships.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of IN SITU conservation?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 15 — Biodiversity and Conservation, Section 15.3
questions.ncert_line: In situ conservation = conservation in natural habitat. Biosphere reserves, national parks and sanctuaries are in situ methods.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Biosphere reserve = in situ. Seed banks, zoos, botanical gardens = ex situ conservation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The full form of ZIFT is:

questions.explanation: Correct: A. ZIFT = Zygote Intra Fallopian Transfer. GIFT = Gamete Intra Fallopian Transfer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The standard electrode potential of SHE (Standard Hydrogen Electrode) is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.3
questions.ncert_line: The standard hydrogen electrode (SHE) is the reference electrode and its potential is arbitrarily set to 0.00 V.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. SHE potential = 0.00 V by definition. All other electrode potentials are measured relative to it.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: During electrolysis of dilute H2SO4, the gas liberated at the anode is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.7
questions.ncert_line: At anode (oxidation): 2H2O → O2 + 4H+ + 4e−. Oxygen is liberated at anode during electrolysis of dilute H2SO4.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Anode = oxidation → O2 liberated. Cathode = reduction → H2 liberated.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following INCREASES the conductance of an electrolytic solution?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.4
questions.ncert_line: Conductance increases with increase in ion concentration. More ions = more charge carriers = higher conductance.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. More ions → higher conductance. Temperature increase also increases conductance (opposite of metals).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: According to Faraday's first law of electrolysis, the mass of substance deposited is proportional to:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.7
questions.ncert_line: Faraday's first law: mass deposited (w) ∝ quantity of electricity (Q = It). w = ZIt where Z is electrochemical equivalent.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. w ∝ Q (charge passed). w = ZQ = ZIt.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The molar conductivity of a strong electrolyte at infinite dilution can be determined by:

options.B: Kohlrausch's law of independent migration of ions

questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Electrochemistry, Section 3.5
questions.ncert_line: For strong electrolytes, λ∞m cannot be measured directly (conductance still measurable at high dilution). Kohlrausch's law is used.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Kohlrausch's law: λ∞m = ν+λ∞+ + ν−λ∞−. Used for strong electrolytes at infinite dilution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The rate of a reaction is independent of concentration of reactants. The order of this reaction is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Kinetics, Section 4.2
questions.ncert_line: Zero order reaction: rate = k[A]0 = k. The rate is constant and independent of reactant concentration.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Zero order — rate is constant, independent of concentration. Example: decomposition of NH3 on Pt.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The half-life of a first-order reaction is 14 minutes. The time required for 99% completion is approximately:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Kinetics, Section 4.3
questions.explanation: Correct: C. 99% completion ≈ 6.64 × t1/2 = 6.64 × 14 ≈ 93 min for first order reactions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following statements about activation energy is INCORRECT?

options.B: Higher activation energy means slower reaction
options.D: It is the minimum energy required for reaction

questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Kinetics, Section 4.5
questions.ncert_line: Activation energy (Ea) is always positive — it is the minimum energy barrier that must be overcome for reaction.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Activation energy is ALWAYS positive (energy barrier). It cannot be negative.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The Arrhenius equation is k = Ae^(−Ea/RT). Here 'A' represents:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Kinetics, Section 4.5
questions.ncert_line: In Arrhenius equation, A is the frequency factor (pre-exponential factor) representing collision frequency.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. A = frequency factor (pre-exponential factor). Ea = activation energy, R = gas constant, T = temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: For the reaction 2A → B, the rate of disappearance of A is 0.04 mol/L/s. The rate of formation of B is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Kinetics, Section 4.1
questions.ncert_line: Rate = −(1/2)d[A]/dt = d[B]/dt. Rate of B formation = (1/2) × rate of A disappearance = 0.04/2 = 0.02 mol/L/s.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Rate of B = 0.02 mol/L/s. Since 2 moles A give 1 mole B, rate of B = half rate of A disappearance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is a colligative property?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.4
questions.ncert_line: Colligative properties depend on number of solute particles. Osmotic pressure, boiling point elevation, freezing point depression and vapour pressure lowering are colligative.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Osmotic pressure is a colligative property. It depends on mole fraction of solute, not its nature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The Van't Hoff factor (i) for Na2SO4 which dissociates completely in water is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.5
questions.ncert_line: Na2SO4 → 2Na+ + SO42−. Total particles = 3. Van't Hoff factor i = 3 for complete dissociation.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Na2SO4 gives 3 ions (2Na+ + SO42−), so i = 3.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following pairs of liquids is IMMISCIBLE?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.1
questions.ncert_line: Water (polar) and benzene (non-polar) are immiscible. Like dissolves like — polar dissolves polar.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Water and benzene are immiscible — polar and non-polar liquids don't mix.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Henry's law states that the solubility of a gas in a liquid is:

options.B: Inversely proportional to partial pressure of gas
options.C: Directly proportional to partial pressure of gas

questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.2
questions.ncert_line: Henry's law: p = KH × x. Solubility (mole fraction x) = p/KH. Solubility is directly proportional to partial pressure.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Henry's law: solubility ∝ partial pressure of gas above solution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Osmotic pressure of a 0.1 M glucose solution at 27°C is approximately: (R = 0.0821 L atm mol−1 K−1)
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Solutions, Section 2.4
questions.ncert_line: π = iCRT = 1 × 0.1 × 0.0821 × 300 = 2.463 atm ≈ 2.46 atm. Glucose doesn't dissociate so i=1.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Lucas test is used to distinguish between:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 11 — Alcohols, Phenols and Ethers, Section 11.3
questions.ncert_line: Lucas test (ZnCl2 + conc. HCl) distinguishes 1°, 2° and 3° alcohols. 3° reacts immediately, 2° in 5 min, 1° doesn't react at room temp.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Lucas test distinguishes 1°, 2°, 3° alcohols. 3° alcohol reacts immediately giving turbidity.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following does NOT give a positive Tollens' test?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 12 — Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids, Section 12.3
questions.ncert_line: Tollens' test (silver mirror test) is positive for aldehydes. Ketones like acetone do NOT reduce Tollens' reagent.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Acetone (ketone) gives NEGATIVE Tollens' test. All aldehydes give positive (silver mirror).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The IUPAC name of CH3CHO is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 12 — Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids, Section 12.1
questions.ncert_line: CH3CHO = acetaldehyde. IUPAC name: ethanal (2 carbons, aldehyde functional group -al).
questions.explanation: Correct: B. CH3CHO = Ethanal. Methanal = HCHO (formaldehyde). Propanal = CH3CH2CHO.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which reagent converts an aldehyde to a primary alcohol?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 12 — Aldehydes, Ketones and Carboxylic Acids, Section 12.4
questions.ncert_line: LiAlH4 (lithium aluminium hydride) reduces aldehydes to primary alcohols and ketones to secondary alcohols.
questions.explanation: Correct: A. LiAlH4 reduces RCHO → RCH2OH (primary alcohol). Tollens'/Fehling's oxidise aldehydes.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is the strongest base among amines?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 13 — Amines, Section 13.2
questions.ncert_line: In aqueous solution, basicity order of aliphatic amines: (CH3)2NH > CH3NH2 > (CH3)3N > NH3 > C6H5NH2.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Dimethylamine (2° amine) is strongest base in aqueous solution due to +I effect and solvation.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which gas is produced when concentrated H2SO4 reacts with copper?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — The p-Block Elements, Section 7.9
questions.ncert_line: Cu + 2H2SO4(conc.) → CuSO4 + SO2 + 2H2O. Hot concentrated H2SO4 is an oxidising acid — produces SO2 with Cu.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. SO2 is produced. Dilute H2SO4 does not react with Cu (below H in activity series).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The hybridisation of sulphur in SF6 is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — The p-Block Elements, Section 7.9
questions.ncert_line: SF6: S has 6 bond pairs and 0 lone pairs → octahedral geometry → sp3d2 hybridisation.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. SF6 = sp3d2 hybridisation. 6 S-F bonds, octahedral shape, bond angle 90°.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following oxides of nitrogen is an anhydride of nitric acid (HNO3)?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — The p-Block Elements, Section 7.8
questions.ncert_line: N2O5 + H2O → 2HNO3. N2O5 is the anhydride of HNO3. N2O3 is the anhydride of HNO2.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. N2O5 is the acid anhydride of HNO3. N2O3 is the anhydride of HNO2.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: The d and f Block Elements
questions.question: Which of the following transition metals has the highest melting point?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 8 — The d and f Block Elements, Section 8.2
questions.ncert_line: Tungsten (W) has the highest melting point of all metals — 3422°C, due to strong metallic bonding from unpaired d electrons.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Tungsten has the highest melting point (3422°C) among all metals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: The d and f Block Elements
questions.question: KMnO4 acts as an oxidising agent in acidic medium. Mn is reduced from +7 to:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 8 — The d and f Block Elements, Section 8.4
questions.ncert_line: In acidic medium: MnO4− + 8H+ + 5e− → Mn2+ + 4H2O. Mn goes from +7 to +2.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. In acidic medium KMnO4 is reduced to Mn2+ (+7 → +2). In neutral/basic medium → MnO2 (+4).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The IUPAC name of [Co(NH3)6]Cl3 is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 9 — Coordination Compounds, Section 9.3
questions.ncert_line: IUPAC: name ligands alphabetically, then metal with oxidation state, then counter ion. [Co(NH3)6]Cl3 = hexaamminecobalt(III) chloride.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Hexaamminecobalt(III) chloride. Ligands named first, metal with OS in Roman numerals, anion last.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The geometry of [Ni(CN)4]2− is square planar because:

options.B: CN− is a strong field ligand causing d8 configuration to adopt square planar
options.D: Ni has completely filled d orbitals

questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 9 — Coordination Compounds, Section 9.5
questions.ncert_line: CN− is a strong field ligand. For d8 Ni2+, strong field causes pairing → square planar with dsp2 hybridisation.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. CN− strong field + d8 Ni2+ → square planar (dsp2). Weak field d8 → tetrahedral (sp3).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following complexes shows optical isomerism?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 9 — Coordination Compounds, Section 9.6
questions.ncert_line: [Co(en)3]3+ with three bidentate en ligands is chiral — it has non-superimposable mirror images (Δ and Λ forms).
questions.explanation: Correct: B. [Co(en)3]3+ shows optical isomerism (Δ and Λ). It has no plane of symmetry.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The EAN (Effective Atomic Number) rule was given by:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Chemistry, Chapter 9 — Coordination Compounds, Section 9.1
questions.ncert_line: The EAN rule (effective atomic number rule) was proposed by N.V. Sidgwick. It states complexes are stable when metal attains noble gas configuration.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Sidgwick proposed EAN rule. Werner proposed coordination theory.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A body of mass 5 kg is acted upon by two perpendicular forces 8 N and 6 N. The resultant acceleration is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion, Section 5.2
questions.ncert_line: Resultant F = √(8²+6²) = 10 N. a = F/m = 10/5 = 2 m/s².
questions.explanation: Correct: A. F_net = 10 N, a = 10/5 = 2 m/s².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A bullet of mass 10 g moving at 400 m/s is stopped in 0.02 s. The average retarding force is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion, Section 5.3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A horse pulls a cart. By Newton's 3rd law, cart pulls horse back equally. Why does the system move forward?

options.B: Action-reaction act on different bodies; ground pushes horse forward
options.C: Newton's 3rd law does not apply here

questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 5 — Laws of Motion, Section 5.4
questions.ncert_line: Action and reaction act on different bodies. Ground friction on horse's hooves gives net forward force.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Action-reaction on different bodies. Net external force on horse-cart system is ground friction on horse.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: At the highest point of a vertical throw, the kinetic energy of the body is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 6 — Work Energy and Power, Section 6.2
questions.ncert_line: At highest point, v = 0 so KE = ½mv² = 0.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. KE = 0 at highest point. All energy converted to PE.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A spring of spring constant 1000 N/m is compressed by 5 cm. The potential energy stored is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 6 — Work Energy and Power, Section 6.4

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The moment of inertia of a thin circular ring of mass M and radius R about its central axis is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 7 — System of Particles and Rotational Motion, Section 7.5
questions.ncert_line: All mass at distance R from axis. I = MR².
questions.explanation: Correct: B. I = MR² for ring. For solid disc I = MR²/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For a solid sphere rolling without slipping, the ratio of rotational KE to total KE is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 7 — System of Particles and Rotational Motion, Section 7.7
questions.ncert_line: KE_rot = (1/5)Mv², KE_total = (7/10)Mv². Ratio = 2/7.
questions.explanation: Correct: A. KE_rot/KE_total = 2/7 for solid sphere.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Angular momentum of a system is conserved when:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 7 — System of Particles and Rotational Motion, Section 7.6
questions.ncert_line: L = Iω is conserved when net external torque = 0.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Net torque = 0 → angular momentum conserved. (Like net force = 0 → linear momentum conserved.)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The escape velocity from Earth's surface is approximately:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 8 — Gravitation, Section 8.5
questions.ncert_line: ve = √(2gR) ≈ 11.2 km/s. Orbital velocity = 7.9 km/s.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Escape velocity = 11.2 km/s. Orbital velocity = 7.9 km/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: If Earth's radius is halved keeping mass constant, escape velocity becomes:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 8 — Gravitation, Section 8.5
questions.explanation: Correct: C. ve ∝ 1/√R. Halving R multiplies ve by √2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: For an isothermal process involving an ideal gas:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.5
questions.ncert_line: Isothermal: T constant → ΔU = 0. First law: Q = ΔU + W = W.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Isothermal: ΔU = 0, all heat converts to work (Q = W).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When an ideal gas is compressed adiabatically, its temperature:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.5
questions.ncert_line: Adiabatic: Q = 0. Work done ON gas increases internal energy → temperature rises.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Adiabatic compression → temperature rises. TV^(γ-1) = constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Efficiency of a Carnot engine between 127°C and 27°C is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 12 — Thermodynamics, Section 12.7

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Electric field inside a uniformly charged hollow spherical shell is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 1 — Electric Charges and Fields, Section 1.14
questions.ncert_line: By Gauss's law, enclosed charge inside shell = 0, so E = 0.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. E = 0 inside hollow shell. All charge on surface.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Two capacitors 3 μF and 6 μF in series. Equivalent capacitance is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 2 — Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance, Section 2.14

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Energy stored in a capacitor C charged to voltage V is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 2 — Electrostatic Potential and Capacitance, Section 2.13
questions.ncert_line: U = ½CV² = Q²/2C = QV/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Wheatstone bridge is balanced when:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.14
questions.ncert_line: Balanced condition: P/Q = R/S. Galvanometer reads zero.
questions.explanation: Correct: A. P/Q = R/S for balanced Wheatstone bridge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Three resistors 2Ω, 3Ω and 6Ω in parallel. Equivalent resistance is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.10

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is of the order of:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 3 — Current Electricity, Section 3.4
questions.ncert_line: Drift velocity ≈ 10⁻³ m/s (mm/s). Thermal speed ≈ 10⁶ m/s.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Drift velocity ~ 10⁻³ m/s. Very slow — current propagates at speed of EM field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Force on a charged particle moving parallel to a magnetic field is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4 — Moving Charges and Magnetism, Section 4.3
questions.ncert_line: F = qvB sinθ. θ = 0° → F = 0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A proton and alpha particle enter same magnetic field with same velocity. Ratio of radii r_p : r_α is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 4 — Moving Charges and Magnetism, Section 4.4

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Lenz's law is a direct consequence of conservation of:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 6 — Electromagnetic Induction, Section 6.5
questions.ncert_line: Induced current opposes change (Lenz) — ensures no free energy is created.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Lenz's law: energy conservation. Induced EMF opposes cause.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A convex lens (f = 20 cm) forms a real image at 60 cm. Object distance is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 9 — Ray Optics, Section 9.5
questions.explanation: Correct: A. Lens formula gives u = −30 cm (30 cm from lens).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In YDSE, when apparatus is immersed in water, fringe width:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.3
questions.ncert_line: β = λD/d. In water λ_water = λ/n < λ_air → fringe width decreases.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. λ decreases in water → fringe width β = λD/d decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In YDSE with slits 0.5 mm apart, screen 1 m away, λ = 500 nm. Fringe width is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 10 — Wave Optics, Section 10.3

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The photoelectric effect was explained by:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 11 — Dual Nature of Radiation, Section 11.3
questions.ncert_line: Einstein (1905) explained photoelectric effect using photon concept. Nobel Prize 1921.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Einstein explained photoelectric effect using E = hν for photons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Dual Nature of Radiation and Matter
questions.question: The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through potential V is proportional to:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 11 — Dual Nature of Radiation, Section 11.5
questions.explanation: Correct: C. λ = h/√(2meV) ∝ 1/√V. More voltage → more energy → shorter wavelength.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Bohr's model, radius of nth orbit is proportional to:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 12 — Atoms, Section 12.3
questions.ncert_line: r_n = n²a₀ where a₀ = 0.529 Å.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. r_n ∝ n². r₁ = 0.529 Å, r₂ = 4 × 0.529 Å.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Binding energy per nucleon is maximum for:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 13 — Nuclei, Section 13.4
questions.ncert_line: Fe-56 has highest binding energy per nucleon (~8.8 MeV) — most stable nucleus.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Fe-56 most stable. Nuclei lighter or heavier than Fe can release energy by fusion or fission.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: The depletion region in a p-n junction forms due to:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 14 — Semiconductor Electronics, Section 14.5
questions.ncert_line: Diffusion of holes (p→n) and electrons (n→p) creates depletion region with immobile ions.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Diffusion of majority carriers creates depletion region.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The acrosome of a sperm is derived from:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.2
questions.ncert_line: The acrosome is derived from the Golgi apparatus and contains hydrolytic enzymes for penetrating the egg.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Acrosome = modified Golgi body. Contains enzymes for fertilisation.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The Graafian follicle ruptures and releases the ovum during:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.3
questions.ncert_line: Ovulation occurs around day 14 of a 28-day cycle. LH surge triggers rupture of Graafian follicle.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Ovulation = rupture of Graafian follicle triggered by LH surge on day ~14.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following hormones is NOT secreted by the placenta?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 3 — Human Reproduction, Section 3.4
questions.ncert_line: Placenta secretes hCG, estrogen, progesterone and hPL. FSH is secreted by anterior pituitary, not placenta.
questions.explanation: Correct: D. FSH is NOT secreted by placenta. Placenta secretes hCG, estrogens, progestogens, hPL.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: MTP stands for:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 4 — Reproductive Health, Section 4.2
questions.ncert_line: MTP = Medical Termination of Pregnancy. It is legal in India up to 20 weeks of pregnancy.
questions.explanation: Correct: A. MTP = Medical Termination of Pregnancy. Legal in India under MTP Act.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Double fertilisation is a characteristic feature of:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.7
questions.ncert_line: Double fertilisation — unique to angiosperms: one sperm + egg = zygote; other sperm + polar nuclei = endosperm.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Double fertilisation is unique to angiosperms. Syngamy + triple fusion.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The enzyme used to join two DNA fragments is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology Principles and Processes, Section 11.2
questions.ncert_line: DNA ligase seals the nicks and joins two DNA fragments by forming phosphodiester bonds.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. DNA ligase = molecular glue. Restriction enzymes cut; ligase joins.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Restriction endonucleases cut DNA at specific sequences called:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 11 — Biotechnology Principles and Processes, Section 11.2
questions.ncert_line: Restriction enzymes recognise specific palindromic sequences (read same in both directions 5'→3') and cut at or near them.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Restriction enzymes cut at palindromic sequences. Example: EcoRI cuts at GAATTC.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Golden Rice is a transgenic variety that is rich in:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and its Applications, Section 12.2
questions.ncert_line: Golden Rice is engineered to produce beta-carotene (precursor of Vitamin A) in the endosperm.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Golden Rice has beta-carotene — a provitamin A. Developed to combat Vitamin A deficiency.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The Bt toxin from Bacillus thuringiensis is coded by the gene:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and its Applications, Section 12.2
questions.ncert_line: The cry genes encode crystal proteins (Bt toxins). CryIAb controls corn borer; CryIAc controls cotton bollworm.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. cry genes code for Bt toxin (crystal protein). nif = nitrogen fixation. bar = herbicide resistance.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The first clinical gene therapy was given for the disease:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 12 — Biotechnology and its Applications, Section 12.3
questions.ncert_line: The first gene therapy (1990) was for ADA deficiency (SCID). Functional ADA gene inserted into patient's lymphocytes.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. ADA deficiency (SCID) — first gene therapy in 1990. ADA enzyme is critical for immune function.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The causative organism of malaria is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 8 — Human Health and Disease, Section 8.2
questions.ncert_line: Malaria is caused by Plasmodium (P. vivax, P. falciparum, P. malariae, P. ovale). Transmitted by female Anopheles mosquito.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Plasmodium causes malaria. Female Anopheles = vector. P. falciparum = most dangerous.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The haemozoin released during malarial attack causes:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 8 — Human Health and Disease, Section 8.2
questions.ncert_line: When RBCs rupture, haemozoin (toxic pigment) is released causing chill and high fever characteristic of malaria.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Haemozoin causes chill and fever. Released when RBCs rupture during merozoite release.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Streptokinase produced by Streptococcus is used as:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.4
questions.ncert_line: Streptokinase (clot buster / thrombolytic agent) produced by Streptococcus is used to dissolve blood clots in heart attack patients.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Streptokinase = clot buster. Used in myocardial infarction treatment.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following microorganisms is used in the production of Swiss cheese?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 10 — Microbes in Human Welfare, Section 10.1
questions.ncert_line: Propionibacterium shermanii produces CO2 during Swiss cheese ripening, creating the characteristic holes.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Propionibacterium shermanii makes Swiss cheese holes by producing CO2.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is a secondary lymphoid organ?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 8 — Human Health and Disease, Section 8.1
questions.ncert_line: Secondary lymphoid organs: spleen, lymph nodes, tonsils, Peyer's patches, appendix. Primary: bone marrow, thymus.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Spleen is a secondary lymphoid organ. Bone marrow and thymus are primary lymphoid organs.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The number of radial nodes in 3s orbital is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Structure of Atom, Section 2.6
questions.ncert_line: Radial nodes = n − l − 1. For 3s: n=3, l=0. Radial nodes = 3−0−1 = 2.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Radial nodes = n−l−1 = 3−0−1 = 2 for 3s orbital.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The correct set of quantum numbers for the valence electron of sodium (Na, Z=11) is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 2 — Structure of Atom, Section 2.6
questions.ncert_line: Na configuration: [Ne]3s¹. Valence electron in 3s: n=3, l=0, m_l=0, m_s=+½.
questions.explanation: Correct: A. Na valence electron in 3s¹: n=3, l=0, m=0, s=+½.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The shape of PCl5 molecule is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Bonding, Section 4.5
questions.ncert_line: PCl5: P has 5 bond pairs, 0 lone pairs → trigonal bipyramidal shape, sp3d hybridisation.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. PCl5 = trigonal bipyramidal, sp3d hybridisation. Bond angles 90° and 120°.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following has the highest bond order?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 4 — Chemical Bonding, Section 4.7
questions.ncert_line: Bond order: N2=3, O2=2, F2=1, Cl2=1. N2 has triple bond (highest bond order) and strongest, shortest bond.
questions.explanation: Correct: A. N2 bond order = 3 (triple bond). Highest bond order = strongest bond.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: For a spontaneous reaction at constant T and P, the Gibbs free energy change (ΔG) must be:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 6 — Thermodynamics, Section 6.5
questions.ncert_line: ΔG < 0 for spontaneous reactions at constant T and P. ΔG = ΔH − TΔS.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. ΔG < 0 for spontaneous process. ΔG = 0 at equilibrium. ΔG > 0 for non-spontaneous.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: For the reaction N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3, if pressure is increased, the equilibrium shifts:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — Equilibrium, Section 7.7
questions.ncert_line: Le Chatelier's principle: increasing pressure favours side with fewer moles of gas. Products (2 mol) < reactants (4 mol).
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Increased pressure → equilibrium shifts to fewer moles of gas → towards NH3 (products).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The pH of a 0.001 M HCl solution is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — Equilibrium, Section 7.10
questions.ncert_line: HCl is a strong acid, fully dissociates. [H+] = 0.001 = 10⁻³ M. pH = −log[H+] = 3.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following salts will give an acidic solution on dissolving in water?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 7 — Equilibrium, Section 7.11
questions.ncert_line: NH4Cl = salt of weak base (NH3) + strong acid (HCl). NH4+ hydrolyses: NH4+ + H2O → NH3 + H3O+. pH < 7.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. NH4Cl gives acidic solution. Salt of weak base + strong acid → acidic.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which property increases regularly across a period from left to right?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Classification of Elements, Section 3.6
questions.ncert_line: Electronegativity increases across a period (left to right) as nuclear charge increases with constant shielding.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Electronegativity increases left to right across a period. Atomic radius decreases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: The element with the highest electronegativity is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Chemistry, Chapter 3 — Classification of Elements, Section 3.6
questions.ncert_line: Fluorine has the highest electronegativity (3.98 on Pauling scale) of all elements.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Fluorine = most electronegative element (3.98). Oxygen = 3.44, Nitrogen = 3.04.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: At resonance in a series LCR circuit, the impedance is equal to:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 7 — Alternating Current, Section 7.8
questions.ncert_line: At resonance: XL = XC, so they cancel. Impedance Z = √(R² + (XL−XC)²) = √(R²) = R.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. At resonance Z = R. Current is maximum at resonance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Power dissipated in a pure inductor connected to AC source is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 7 — Alternating Current, Section 7.5
questions.ncert_line: Pure inductor: phase difference between V and I = 90°. Power = VIcos90° = 0.
questions.explanation: Correct: C. Pure inductor dissipates zero power. Current leads/lags voltage by 90°.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which of the following electromagnetic waves has the highest frequency?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8 — Electromagnetic Waves, Section 8.3
questions.ncert_line: Frequency order: γ-rays > X-rays > UV > visible > IR > microwave > radio waves.
questions.explanation: Correct: D. Gamma rays have highest frequency (and energy, shortest wavelength).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which EM radiation is used in RADAR?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Physics, Chapter 8 — Electromagnetic Waves, Section 8.3
questions.ncert_line: RADAR (Radio Detection And Ranging) uses microwaves (wavelength ~ cm range).
questions.explanation: Correct: C. RADAR uses microwaves. IR used in remote sensing/night vision. UV used in sterilisation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: According to the law of equipartition of energy, energy per degree of freedom per molecule is:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 11 Physics, Chapter 13 — Kinetic Theory, Section 13.5
questions.ncert_line: Law of equipartition: each degree of freedom contributes ½kT to average energy of a molecule.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Energy per degree of freedom = ½kT. Monatomic gas (3 dof): total = 3/2 kT.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: The process by which pollen grains are transferred from anther to stigma is called:
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.3
questions.ncert_line: Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from the anther to the stigma of a flower.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Pollination = pollen transfer anther→stigma. Fertilisation = fusion of gametes.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a feature of cleistogamous flowers?
questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.3
questions.ncert_line: Cleistogamous flowers never open and are therefore always self-pollinated, not cross-pollinated.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Cleistogamous flowers are self-pollinated (not cross-pollinated). Example: Commelina, Viola.

questions.subject: Biology
questions.question: Double fertilisation in angiosperms involves fusion of:

options.A: Two sperms with two egg cells
options.B: One sperm with egg and one sperm with secondary nucleus
options.C: One sperm with two polar nuclei only
options.D: Two sperms with the central cell

questions.ncert_ref: Class 12 Biology, Chapter 2 — Sexual Reproduction in Flowering Plants, Section 2.5
questions.ncert_line: Double fertilisation: one sperm fuses with egg (syngamy) → zygote; second sperm fuses with secondary nucleus (triple fusion) → endosperm.
questions.explanation: Correct: B. Syngamy: sperm + egg → zygote. Triple fusion: sperm + 2 polar nuclei → primary endosperm nucleus.
